title: The internet after AI still feels unfinished to me
summary: A closing essay on the internet after AI, written from the feeling that the medium is still deciding what it wants to become.
topic: the internet after AI when clarity started to feel like rebellion
body: There was a version of the internet after AI circulating in April 2026 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. The trend sounds obvious only because somebody has already done the quiet work of narrowing the scene. Standing inside a Sunday morning with fewer tabs open than usual, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

I keep finding the topic interesting because it refuses to line up cleanly with anyone’s preferred camp. The internet after AI can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I take the question to be about what kinds of obedience and attention the tool normalizes under the cover of convenience.

In 2026, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not merely adopting systems, they were trying on identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted a break from work that had become repetitive, tedious, or just plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. The trouble begins when a feeling gets promoted into an institution, because institutions do not disappear as easily as enthusiasm does.

One thing I watch for now is what disappears once a story starts getting repeated in polished settings. Usually the missing pieces are familiar: labor, repair, emotional load, error handling, governance, and who ends up carrying the inconvenience. The internet after AI looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had looked sleek at first started to read more like a stack of trade-offs wearing good marketing. That does not make the thing useless. Sometimes making a system legible again is the first real job criticism does well.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It shows me the technologies, yes, but also the evolution of what I considered worth noticing. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. The internet after AI sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from April 2026: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. The part I keep returning to is not the prediction but the burden placed on the people expected to absorb the tool into daily life. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: Slow writing is one way to stay visible to yourself
summary: A note on slow writing as a defense against ambient convenience and flattened thought.
topic: slow writing while trying to keep a human scale
body: I wrote this down in April 2026 after thinking about slow writing from the vantage point of a small desk in good light. I was less interested in the headline claim than in the older question beneath it, what kind of world does this thing presume is already waiting? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. The conversation tends to favor capability over context, performance over maintenance, and scale over the simple question of livability. Slow writing seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism I cared about was mostly off to the side of the official explanation. The conversation in public usually starts with whatever can be pointed to most easily: interface, model, dashboard, robot. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They quietly decide who may stay general and who must become machine-readable. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, slow writing stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. Because it resists tidy measurement, that difference tends to vanish from the official accounting. But mood is one of the outputs. That kind of narrowing effect is part of what the system is, even if nobody wanted to advertise it. It is part of what the product is. Arguments that dismiss human texture as softness next to performance have never done much for me.

Looking back from April 2026, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “What trustworthy interfaces taught me about building futures,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. What held me here was the post-demo layer: upkeep, political assumptions, quiet social sorting, and the emotional terms on which people were being asked to cooperate with the system. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was an optimism with too little contact behind it, not a bad instinct so much as a premature one.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. Good tools deserve scrutiny too, especially about the kind of world they make easier to inhabit. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The sane version usually starts by distrusting the oversized story until the smaller one has earned the right to expand. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “The problem with safety debates is usually how novelty can impersonate depth.”

A final note to myself from April 2026: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. These days I care less about winning the prediction game than about noticing what a tool asks of the people standing closest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: A trustworthy interface should make the right thing easier to see
summary: An essay on trustworthy interfaces and the calm that comes from seeing the right thing at the right moment.
topic: trustworthy interfaces after years of moving too quickly
body: My notes on trustworthy interfaces began with a small irritation in March 2026. What unsettled me was not disagreement but how easily everyone seemed to agree on the headline version of events. A sentence that lands too neatly usually owes its neatness to missing context. In this case the omitted piece was the social cost of never pausing to notice what changed. That may sound abstract, but it shows up in very concrete places: maintenance, blame, sequencing, and who is asked to trust first.

Trustworthy interfaces appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The technical side is not hard to describe, mostly because it arrives with numbers, diagrams, and product theater already attached. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Tools are never just functions, they come with built-in assumptions about the user and the world around that user. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also about the atmosphere the system normalizes while everyone is busy asking whether it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is one thing in a prototype and another in schools, transit, hospitals, housing, and workplaces where people inherit the system as environment. The social cost of never pausing to notice what changed becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

My feeling about the topic stayed mostly intact, but my tolerance for vague phrasing did not. After writing “On online identity when the novelty tax has mostly been paid,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. A claim that cannot make it through ordinary life, through fatigue, tools, incentives, and time pressure, is probably not built to last. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. The key is to watch for the moment utility turns into dependence and weirdness gets rebranded as inevitability. Trustworthy interfaces seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. What becomes durable is often less the tool than the pattern of relationship it makes routine.

A final note to myself from March 2026: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. The part I keep returning to is not the prediction but the burden placed on the people expected to absorb the tool into daily life. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

The headline version of the topic interested me less than the sturdier question underneath it: what kind of world is this idea imagining into existence? I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was the sort of incomplete optimism you get before the machinery has had time to reach enough people.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. I do not see any contradiction between admiring a tool and interrogating the world it quietly normalizes. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The healthy move is to keep the claim narrow until something real forces it to widen. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “I keep coming back to image models.”

A final note to myself from January 2026: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. title: Reliability is what remains after the demo fades
summary: A note on reliability after the demo, when the real question becomes what still holds under repetition.
topic: model reliability while trying to keep a human scale
body: My notes on model reliability began with a small irritation in January 2026. The consensus arrived so smoothly that it started to feel like a warning sign rather than a comfort. When a consensus sounds too polished, I start asking what was trimmed away to make it travel so well. In this case the omitted piece was what remains when convenience becomes ambient. What seems abstract at altitude gets specific very quickly once you ask who maintains the thing, who takes the hit when it fails, and who benefits from the trust it extracts.

Model reliability appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering half is always easier to narrate because it collapses neatly into components, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Tools are never just functions, they come with built-in assumptions about the user and the world around that user. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also about the habits, dependencies, and moods the system quietly teaches while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is very different to push the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface photographs well. What remains when convenience becomes ambient becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

The real shift was not in my interest but in how little abstraction I was willing to tolerate. After writing “I keep coming back to interface trust,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. A claim that cannot make it through ordinary life, through fatigue, tools, incentives, and time pressure, is probably not built to last. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. The part worth watching is the slide from convenience into obligation, especially when the slide is narrated as progress. Model reliability seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. Usually the deeper change is a social relationship that hardens into routine before anyone names it clearly.

A final note to myself from January 2026: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I no longer find the forecast itself as interesting as the human bargain each tool quietly proposes to its nearest users. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: The trouble with digital twins is how quickly they become authoritative
summary: This one looks at digital twins as instruments of authority as much as mirrors of reality.
topic: digital twins once maintenance became the real product
body: There was a version of digital twins circulating in December 2025 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. More often it is a story assembled only after someone has chosen the conditions that make the trend look inevitable. Standing inside a train ride where the future looked mostly like error handling, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

I keep finding the topic interesting because it refuses to line up cleanly with anyone’s preferred camp. Digital twins can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I take the question to be about what kinds of obedience and attention the tool normalizes under the cover of convenience.

In 2025, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were using the systems partly as tools and partly as ways of auditioning who they wanted to be. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some were just tired of tasks that felt repetitive, exhausting, and beneath anyone’s best attention. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. The trouble begins when a feeling gets promoted into an institution, because institutions do not disappear as easily as enthusiasm does.

Over time I have learned to watch what drops out of a story once people begin repeating it for a living. Most of the missing pieces come from the same family: labor, repair, emotional burden, governance, failure handling, and uneven inconvenience. Digital twins looked different to me once I framed it that way. The elegant solution started to look less elegant and more like a pile of trade-offs with excellent styling. That does not make the thing useless. Often the first honest service criticism provides is simple legibility.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It shows me both the movement of the technologies and the movement of my own attention. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Digital twins sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from December 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I no longer find the forecast itself as interesting as the human bargain each tool quietly proposes to its nearest users. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: Personal AI gets worse the moment it becomes generic
summary: A post about personal AI and the way generic product logic can ruin something meant to feel specific.
topic: personal AI after the magic show
body: I wrote this down in December 2025 after thinking about personal AI from the vantage point of a desktop crowded with small useful automations. The surface topic was not the real hook for me. The better question was what kind of life the idea seemed to assume in advance. With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We default to capability talk, then performance talk, then scale talk, while the livability question gets deferred again. Personal AI seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

I kept getting pulled toward a different mechanism than the one the public conversation liked to emphasize. When people talk in public they reach first for the visible layer, the thing that demos cleanly and fits in a headline. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change the social split between people who get to stay complex and people who must become easy for the machine to parse. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, personal AI stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. It slips past the benchmark view of the world, which is one reason people discount it. But mood is one of the outputs. A system that steadily constricts attention or multiplies little permissions is telling you something honest about itself. It is part of what the product is. The moment someone treats human texture as optional, I stop trusting the frame they are using.

Looking back from December 2025, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “I keep coming back to interface trust,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. I was paying more attention now to the afterlife of the system, the maintenance, the politics, the sorting logic, and the emotional terms of participation. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was incomplete in the familiar way optimism becomes incomplete before the machinery has touched enough real lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can like a tool and still ask what sort of world it is slowly arranging around itself. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The better habit is to keep the frame modest until the evidence actually asks for something bigger. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “A note on chat interfaces before the story settled.”

A final note to myself from December 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. The part I keep returning to is not the prediction but the burden placed on the people expected to absorb the tool into daily life. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

Standing inside an office where the exciting part was no longer the model but the glue, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of the appeal is that the topic will not stay obedient inside a simple ideological frame. Local inference can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I take the question to be about what kinds of obedience and attention the tool normalizes under the cover of convenience.

In 2025, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were using the systems partly as tools and partly as ways of auditioning who they wanted to be. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some people simply wanted relief from work that had become exhausting, repetitive, or obviously wasteful. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. The trouble begins when a feeling gets promoted into an institution, because institutions do not disappear as easily as enthusiasm does.

Over time I have learned to watch what drops out of a story once people begin repeating it for a living. The omissions are almost always ordinary but important: labor, maintenance, repair, emotional load, governance, and uneven cost. Local inference looked different to me once I framed it that way. The elegant solution started to look less elegant and more like a pile of trade-offs with excellent styling. That does not make the thing useless. Often the first honest service criticism provides is simple legibility.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It helps me track not only the tools but the slow education of my own noticing. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Local inference sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from September 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. At this point the future matters to me mostly as a way of reading the demands a tool places on the people closest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: I trust agents more when they look a little unfinished
summary: A personal note on agents, reliability, and why unfinished-looking systems can be easier to trust.
topic: agents once maintenance became the real product
body: I wrote this down in September 2025 after thinking about agents from the vantage point of a train ride where the future looked mostly like error handling. I was less interested in the headline claim than in the older question beneath it, what kind of world does this thing presume is already waiting? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. Most discussions rush from capability to performance to scale and leave the question of livability trailing behind. Agents seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that mattered to me was not the obvious one from the demo version of the story. Public language tends to grab the most visible element first: the model, the dashboard, the robot, the bit that photographs well. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They redraw the line between the people allowed to remain flexible and the people required to become legible to the system. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, agents stop looking like a gadget and start looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That distinction hides from measurement more easily than people expect. But mood is one of the outputs. If the system keeps turning ordinary life into a permission ladder, I assume that outcome belongs to the design. It is part of what the product is. Performance matters, but I have never trusted the argument that treats lived texture as an indulgence.

Looking back from September 2025, I could already feel my own position shifting. I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “The problem with interface trust is usually the strange politics of delegating judgment,” from August 2025. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. What held me here was the post-demo layer: upkeep, political assumptions, quiet social sorting, and the emotional terms on which people were being asked to cooperate with the system. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was an optimism with too little contact behind it, not a bad instinct so much as a premature one.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can like a tool and still ask what sort of world it is slowly arranging around itself. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The better habit is to keep the frame modest until the evidence actually asks for something bigger. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “On open weights as the internet learned a new kind of performance.”

A final note to myself from September 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. These days I care less about winning the prediction game than about noticing what a tool asks of the people standing closest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: The quiet bargain inside ambient assistants
summary: A quieter look at ambient assistants and the subtle trade people make when software starts hovering nearby.
topic: ambient assistants after the magic show
body: My notes on ambient assistants began with a small irritation in August 2025. As soon as everyone started talking as if the meaning were obvious, I felt the need to slow down. Consensus has a habit of sounding elegant only after the inconvenient parts have been sanded off. In this case the omitted piece was the long argument between autonomy and auditability. What seems abstract at altitude gets specific very quickly once you ask who maintains the thing, who takes the hit when it fails, and who benefits from the trust it extracts.

Ambient assistants appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. It is simple enough to talk about the engineering because engineering comes with visible parts, measurable claims, and a nice clean demo culture. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. What arrives with the tool is not just capability but a sketch of the person it expects and the world it would like to make ordinary. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also about the social mood the system smuggles in under the banner of functionality.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another thing entirely to embed that logic in schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and call the result progress because the interface looks polished. The long argument between autonomy and auditability becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

The subject still interested me, but my threshold for abstraction dropped hard. After writing “The problem with interface trust is usually the strange politics of delegating judgment,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. Once a grand claim fails contact with an ordinary day, I assume the problem is with the claim rather than the day. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. Still, it matters to keep an eye on the moment usefulness starts hardening into dependence and novelty gets sold back to us as fate. Ambient assistants seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. Usually it is not one invention but a relationship style that settles in before we have decided whether it suits us.

A final note to myself from August 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. The part I keep returning to is not the prediction but the burden placed on the people expected to absorb the tool into daily life. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was incomplete in the familiar way optimism becomes incomplete before the machinery has touched enough real lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. Good tools deserve scrutiny too, especially about the kind of world they make easier to inhabit. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The healthy move is to keep the claim narrow until something real forces it to widen. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “What research theater taught me about building futures.”

A final note to myself from June 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. title: Most evaluation problems are really trust problems
summary: An essay on evaluation as a trust problem, not just a metrics problem.
topic: evaluation once maintenance became the real product
body: My notes on evaluation began with a small irritation in June 2025. What unsettled me was not disagreement but how easily everyone seemed to agree on the headline version of events. When everyone repeats the same clean formulation, I assume some rough but important detail has been left on the cutting-room floor. In this case the omitted piece was what happens when a product grows more competent than legible. In practice the issue surfaces through plain details, like who keeps the system running, who bears the cost of failure, and who has the least choice about trusting it.

Evaluation appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. Talking about the engineering is straightforward because it already knows how to present itself. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Buried in every tool is an assumption about who will use it well and what kind of social environment should feel natural around it. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also a question of what kinds of dependency and behavior the system makes feel ordinary.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another step entirely to turn that logic into public infrastructure and hope presentation smooths over the burden. What happens when a product grows more competent than legible becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

I did not lose interest in the topic, I lost patience for talking about it at altitude. After writing “On interface trust after the magic show,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a future-facing claim cannot survive contact with somebody's actual day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I stop trusting it pretty quickly. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. The part worth watching is the slide from convenience into obligation, especially when the slide is narrated as progress. Evaluation seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. Most of the time the shift is relational before it is technological, it becomes normal before it becomes discussed.

A final note to myself from June 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I no longer find the forecast itself as interesting as the human bargain each tool quietly proposes to its nearest users. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: Local inference is partly about keeping the machine nearby
summary: An essay on local inference, proximity, and the appeal of systems you can still point to.
topic: local inference after the magic show
body: There was a version of local inference circulating in May 2025 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It often turns out to be a story built around selective conditions rather than a stable reality. Standing inside a desktop crowded with small useful automations, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

The subject stays interesting partly because it resists the usual ideological sorting rituals. Local inference can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I mean asking what the tool is quietly teaching us about attention and compliance while we applaud the low-friction experience.

In 2025, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were using the systems partly as tools and partly as ways of auditioning who they wanted to be. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some people simply wanted relief from work that had become exhausting, repetitive, or obviously wasteful. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. The trouble begins when a feeling gets promoted into an institution, because institutions do not disappear as easily as enthusiasm does.

I have learned to pay attention to what vanishes as soon as a story becomes professionalized. Most of the missing pieces come from the same family: labor, repair, emotional burden, governance, failure handling, and uneven inconvenience. Local inference looked different to me once I framed it that way. The elegant solution started to look less elegant and more like a pile of trade-offs with excellent styling. That does not make the thing useless. Often the first honest service criticism provides is simple legibility.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not just what the technologies were doing, but what I was gradually training myself to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Local inference sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from May 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. What holds my attention now is not who wins the argument about the future, but what the tool demands from the people living beside it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: Agents make responsibility harder to point to
summary: This essay treats agents less as magic workers and more as systems that blur accountability.
topic: agents while everyone tried to make software feel responsible
body: I wrote this down in May 2025 after thinking about agents from the vantage point of a morning spent comparing outputs instead of admiring them. What held me was not the headline version but the deeper question of what sort of world the idea thought it was entering. With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. Most discussions rush from capability to performance to scale and leave the question of livability trailing behind. Agents seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

What interested me most was not the feature everyone reached for in public explanations. When people talk in public they reach first for the visible layer, the thing that demos cleanly and fits in a headline. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They reshape who gets to stay broad and who has to start translating themselves into machine-friendly terms. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, agents stop looking like a gadget and start looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. It is easy to underrate because benchmarks rarely register that kind of difference cleanly. But mood is one of the outputs. That kind of narrowing effect is part of what the system is, even if nobody wanted to advertise it. It is part of what the product is. I have little patience for arguments that reduce human texture to a secondary, sentimental concern.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. I do not see any contradiction between admiring a tool and interrogating the world it quietly normalizes. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. I keep finding the topic interesting because it refuses to line up cleanly with anyone’s preferred camp. Workflow automation can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I mean asking what the tool is quietly teaching us about attention and compliance while we applaud the low-friction experience.

In 2025, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. The systems were functioning as identity stages as much as practical tools. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some were just tired of tasks that felt repetitive, exhausting, and beneath anyone’s best attention. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. A hot take is easy to outgrow. An institution built on the same feeling is not.

One thing I watch for now is what disappears once a story starts getting repeated in polished settings. Usually the missing pieces are familiar: labor, repair, emotional load, error handling, governance, and who ends up carrying the inconvenience. Workflow automation looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had looked sleek at first started to read more like a stack of trade-offs wearing good marketing. That does not make the thing useless. Quite often the first service criticism provides is to restore legibility.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It shows me both the movement of the technologies and the movement of my own attention. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Workflow automation sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from March 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I am less interested now in being right about what comes next than in seeing what a system quietly asks its operators, users, and bystanders to become. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: What an evidence chain is supposed to buy you
summary: A piece about evidence chains and what they are meant to preserve once decisions start moving faster.
topic: evidence chains after the magic show
body: I wrote this down in February 2025 after thinking about evidence chains from the vantage point of a desktop crowded with small useful automations. I kept drifting past the headline toward the quieter question underneath it: what kind of world was this idea written for? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. The conversation tends to favor capability over context, performance over maintenance, and scale over the simple question of livability. Evidence chains seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism I cared about was mostly off to the side of the official explanation. The conversation in public usually starts with whatever can be pointed to most easily: interface, model, dashboard, robot. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change the social split between people who get to stay complex and people who must become easy for the machine to parse. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, evidence chains stop looking like a gadget and start looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That distinction hides from measurement more easily than people expect. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system reliably narrows attention, thickens procedure, or turns daily life into a chain of little permissions, that is part of the product, not collateral damage. It is part of what the product is. Arguments that dismiss human texture as softness next to performance have never done much for me.

Looking back from February 2025, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “What evaluation taught me about building futures,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. What interested me most by then was everything that happened after the applause died down: maintenance, social order, and the emotional bargain tucked inside the workflow. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was the sort of incomplete optimism you get before the machinery has had time to reach enough people.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. I do not see any contradiction between admiring a tool and interrogating the world it quietly normalizes. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. Most serious thinking starts by resisting the urge to inflate the story too early. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “The problem with coordination systems is usually software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit.”

A final note to myself from February 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I am less interested now in being right about what comes next than in seeing what a system quietly asks its operators, users, and bystanders to become. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: Evaluation gets harder right when it starts to matter
summary: A post about evaluation and the uneasy fact that measurement gets harder exactly when people start depending on it.
topic: evaluation while everyone tried to make software feel responsible
body: My notes on evaluation began with a small irritation in February 2025. The speed of the agreement bothered me more than the claim itself. When a consensus sounds too polished, I start asking what was trimmed away to make it travel so well. In this case the omitted piece was the difference between an assistant and a dependency. On the ground it becomes a practical matter of upkeep, error absorption, social risk, and who gets rewarded for calling all of that progress.

Evaluation appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering half is always easier to narrate because it collapses neatly into components, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Tools are never just functions, they come with built-in assumptions about the user and the world around that user. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also about the kinds of behavior and dependency the system teaches people to accept.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is one thing in a prototype and another in schools, transit, hospitals, housing, and workplaces where people inherit the system as environment. The difference between an assistant and a dependency becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

The subject still interested me, but my threshold for abstraction dropped hard. After writing “A note on local inference when reliability turned out to be the whole game,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. Once a grand claim fails contact with an ordinary day, I assume the problem is with the claim rather than the day. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. What matters is noticing the point where a useful thing becomes something you are no longer really free to decline. Evaluation seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. Usually the deeper change is a social relationship that hardens into routine before anyone names it clearly.

A final note to myself from February 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. The part I keep returning to is not the prediction but the burden placed on the people expected to absorb the tool into daily life. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On digital twins while everyone tried to make software feel responsible
summary: A reflection on digital twins and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: digital twins while everyone tried to make software feel responsible
body: My notes on digital twins began with a small irritation in October 2024. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the strange politics of delegating judgment. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Digital twins appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The strange politics of delegating judgment becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “The problem with memory systems is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Digital twins seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from October 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: I keep coming back to personal AI
summary: A post about personal AI as the tools got more useful and less theatrical, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: personal AI as the tools got more useful and less theatrical
body: There was a version of personal AI circulating in October 2024 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside an office where the exciting part was no longer the model but the glue, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Personal AI can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2024, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Personal AI looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Personal AI sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from October 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

topic: interface trust when reliability turned out to be the whole game
body: I wrote this down in September 2024 after thinking about interface trust from the vantage point of a train ride where the future looked mostly like error handling. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Interface trust seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, interface trust stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

A few months earlier I had written “The problem with memory systems is usually the difference between an assistant and a dependency,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “A note on homebound automation when the infrastructure finally became visible.”

A final note to myself from September 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Agents can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

title: I keep coming back to ambient assistants

topic: ambient assistants as the tools got more useful and less theatrical
body: I wrote this down in June 2024 after thinking about ambient assistants from the vantage point of an office where the exciting part was no longer the model but the glue. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Ambient assistants seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, ambient assistants stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “On memory systems while everyone tried to make software feel responsible,” from April 2024. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. title: A note on digital twins when reliability turned out to be the whole game
summary: A reflection on digital twins and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: digital twins when reliability turned out to be the whole game
body: My notes on digital twins began with a small irritation in May 2024. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the long argument between autonomy and auditability. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Digital twins appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The long argument between autonomy and auditability becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “On memory systems while everyone tried to make software feel responsible,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Digital twins seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. A final note to myself from May 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. title: On evaluation while everyone tried to make software feel responsible
summary: A reflection on evaluation and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: evaluation while everyone tried to make software feel responsible
body: I wrote this down in February 2024 after thinking about evaluation from the vantage point of a whiteboard full of edge cases rather than aspirations. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Evaluation seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, evaluation stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from February 2024, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “I keep coming back to local inference,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “The problem with delivery logistics is usually software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit.”

A final note to myself from February 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. title: I keep coming back to local inference

topic: local inference as the tools got more useful and less theatrical
body: My notes on local inference began with a small irritation in February 2024. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was what happens when a product grows more competent than legible. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Local inference appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. title: A note on agents when reliability turned out to be the whole game
summary: A personal essay about agents, and the longer argument hidden inside the long argument between autonomy and auditability.
topic: agents when reliability turned out to be the whole game
body: There was a version of agents circulating in February 2024 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a train ride where the future looked mostly like error handling, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Agents can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2024, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Agents looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Agents sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from February 2024: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. title: On prompt rituals when everyone had a demo but nobody had a shared vocabulary
summary: A personal essay about prompt rituals, and the longer argument hidden inside what gets lost when the demo becomes the argument.
topic: prompt rituals when everyone had a demo but nobody had a shared vocabulary
body: My notes on prompt rituals began with a small irritation in October 2023. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was what gets lost when the demo becomes the argument. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Prompt rituals appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern.
