title: I keep coming back to open weights
summary: An essay on open weights, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: open weights as the internet learned a new kind of performance
body: There was a version of open weights circulating in September 2023 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a browser window full of generated images, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Open weights can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2023, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Open weights looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Open weights sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from September 2023: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: A note on language models while the hype still outran the evidence
summary: A reflection on language models and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: language models while the hype still outran the evidence
body: I wrote this down in September 2023 after thinking about language models from the vantage point of a quiet hour reading papers with too many grand nouns. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Language models seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, language models stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from September 2023, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “The problem with AI search is usually the pressure to form a worldview too quickly,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “The problem with delivery logistics is usually the difference between resilience and improvisation.”

A final note to myself from September 2023: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

body: There was a version of chat interfaces circulating in May 2023 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a group chat melting down over the same screenshot, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

title: I keep coming back to benchmarks
summary: A post about benchmarks as the internet learned a new kind of performance, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: benchmarks as the internet learned a new kind of performance
body: I wrote this down in April 2023 after thinking about benchmarks from the vantage point of a browser window full of generated images. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Benchmarks seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, benchmarks stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from April 2023, I could already feel my own position shifting. I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “On AI search when everyone had a demo but nobody had a shared vocabulary,” from February 2023. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “I keep coming back to telepresence rituals.”

A final note to myself from April 2023: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On AI search as the internet learned a new kind of performance
summary: A personal essay about AI search, and the longer argument hidden inside the pressure to form a worldview too quickly.
topic: AI search as the internet learned a new kind of performance
body: I wrote this down in November 2022 after thinking about AI search from the vantage point of a quiet hour reading papers with too many grand nouns. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. AI search seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, AI search stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from November 2022, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “I keep coming back to tool-using models,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “What homebound automation taught me about building futures.”

A final note to myself from November 2022: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: I keep coming back to tool-using models
summary: An essay on tool-using models, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: tool-using models while the hype still outran the evidence
body: My notes on tool-using models began with a small irritation in November 2022. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the difference between surprise and understanding. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Tool-using models appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The difference between surprise and understanding becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “A note on chat interfaces before the story settled,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Tool-using models seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from November 2022: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: A note on research theater before the story settled
summary: A reflection on research theater and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: research theater before the story settled
body: There was a version of research theater circulating in October 2022 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a coffee shop conversation that turned into an accidental seminar, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Research theater can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2022, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Research theater looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Research theater sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from October 2022: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On open weights as the internet learned a new kind of performance

topic: open weights as the internet learned a new kind of performance
body: My notes on open weights began with a small irritation in May 2022. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the pressure to form a worldview too quickly. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Open weights appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The pressure to form a worldview too quickly becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “The problem with tool-using models is usually how novelty can impersonate depth,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Open weights seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. title: I keep coming back to language models
summary: A post about language models while the hype still outran the evidence, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: language models while the hype still outran the evidence
body: There was a version of language models circulating in April 2022 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside an exhausted midnight walk after too much time online, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Language models can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2022, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Language models looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Language models sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from April 2022: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: A note on AI search before the story settled
summary: A personal essay about AI search, and the longer argument hidden inside what gets lost when the demo becomes the argument.
topic: AI search before the story settled
body: I wrote this down in April 2022 after thinking about AI search from the vantage point of a coffee shop conversation that turned into an accidental seminar. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. AI search seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, AI search stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from April 2022, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “The problem with tool-using models is usually how novelty can impersonate depth,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “The problem with fragile supply chains is usually the difference between resilience and improvisation.”

A final note to myself from April 2022: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On public health dashboards when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite

topic: public health dashboards when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite
body: There was a version of public health dashboards circulating in October 2021 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside an empty downtown street, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Public health dashboards can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2021, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Public health dashboards looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

title: I keep coming back to contactless interfaces
summary: An essay on contactless interfaces, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: contactless interfaces under pressure
body: I wrote this down in September 2021 after thinking about contactless interfaces from the vantage point of a video call where everyone looked slightly ghosted by their own webcam. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Contactless interfaces seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, contactless interfaces stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from September 2021, I could already feel my own position shifting. I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “On mutual aid apps when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite,” from June 2021. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “I keep coming back to desert prototypes.”

A final note to myself from September 2021: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: A note on homebound automation when the infrastructure finally became visible
summary: A reflection on homebound automation and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: homebound automation when the infrastructure finally became visible
body: My notes on homebound automation began with a small irritation in August 2021. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the quiet cost of calling desperation efficiency. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Homebound automation appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The quiet cost of calling desperation efficiency becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “On mutual aid apps when the world stopped pretending slack was infinite,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Homebound automation seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from August 2021: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, coordination systems stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

title: A note on remote education tools after convenience turned into dependency

topic: remote education tools after convenience turned into dependency
body: There was a version of remote education tools circulating in December 2020 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside a late-night browser tab spiral full of charts and shipping estimates, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Remote education tools can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2020, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Remote education tools looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Remote education tools sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

title: On remote presence under pressure
summary: A personal essay about remote presence, and the longer argument hidden inside software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit.
topic: remote presence under pressure
body: My notes on remote presence began with a small irritation in April 2020. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Remote presence appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. Software stepping into spaces that used to belong to habit becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “The problem with fragile supply chains is usually the difference between resilience and improvisation,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Remote presence seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from April 2020: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: I keep coming back to warehouse software
summary: An essay on warehouse software, with one eye on the machinery and one eye on the people asked to live with it.
topic: warehouse software when the infrastructure finally became visible
body: There was a version of warehouse software circulating in March 2020 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside the unnerving quiet of a masked grocery line, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Warehouse software can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2020, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Warehouse software looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Warehouse software sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from March 2020: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: A note on mutual aid apps after convenience turned into dependency
summary: A reflection on mutual aid apps and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: mutual aid apps after convenience turned into dependency
body: I wrote this down in February 2020 after thinking about mutual aid apps from the vantage point of a late-night browser tab spiral full of charts and shipping estimates. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Mutual aid apps seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, mutual aid apps stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from February 2020, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “The problem with fragile supply chains is usually the difference between resilience and improvisation,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “What urban sensors taught me about building futures.”

A final note to myself from February 2020: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On domestic robots as a rehearsal for a different kind of city
summary: A reflection on domestic robots and the kind of world it quietly assumes.
topic: domestic robots as a rehearsal for a different kind of city
body: There was a version of domestic robots circulating in June 2019 that sounded inevitable. I have become suspicious of inevitability as a tone. It is usually a story people tell after they have quietly selected the conditions under which a trend looks obvious. Standing inside an overcaffeinated dinner where everyone had a sketchbook open, what I noticed instead was contingency: how much of the future depends on atmosphere, labor, trust, and timing, and how often those human variables are treated as background noise once a technical idea starts sounding important.

Part of what keeps this topic interesting is that it resists clean ideological sorting. Domestic robots can look emancipatory when you focus on drudgery leaving the picture, and unsettling when you look at how quickly convenience hardens into expectation. Both reactions are real. The adult version of the conversation has to hold them at the same time. I do not think skepticism means refusing tools. I think it means asking what the tool is teaching us about obedience, attention, and scale while we are busy admiring how much friction it has removed.

In 2019, the social texture around all of this mattered as much as the technical substance. People were not just adopting systems; they were auditioning identities through them. Some wanted to look early. Some wanted to look rigorous. Some wanted relief from tasks that had become exhausting, repetitive, or plainly stupid. I sympathized with all of that. But the cultural story was moving faster than the evidence. The risk there is not only bad prediction. It is that you end up building institutions around a feeling, and institutions are much harder to unwind than a hot take in a group chat.

Over time I have learned to pay close attention to what disappears from a story once people start repeating it professionally. Usually it is the same cluster of missing pieces: labor, error handling, emotional load, repair, governance, and the uneven distribution of inconvenience. Domestic robots looked different to me once I framed it that way. What had first appeared as a sleek solution started to read more like a bundle of trade-offs with a persuasive publicist. That does not make the thing useless. It just makes it legible again, which is often the first real service criticism can provide.

Maybe that is the real use of an archive like this one. It lets me see not only what the technologies were doing, but what I was learning to notice. The tools change. The language changes. The market changes its costume every six months. What stays interesting is the slower shift in what feels normal, what feels extractive, and what still feels worth wanting. Domestic robots sat right in the middle of that drift, which is why I kept turning it over until it revealed more than its own sales pitch.

A final note to myself from June 2019: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: On repair culture before venture language flattened the mood
summary: A post about repair culture before venture language flattened the mood, written against the temptation to make the future sound cleaner than it is.
topic: repair culture before venture language flattened the mood
body: I wrote this down in May 2019 after thinking about repair culture from the vantage point of a warehouse party full of hacked machines. What interested me was not the headline version of the topic, but the more durable question underneath it: what sort of world does this idea assume is waiting for it? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. We talk about capability before context, performance before maintenance, scale before whether the thing makes life feel more inhabitable. Repair culture seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism that interested me most was not the one usually highlighted in public. Public language likes the visible move: the robot, the interface, the model, the service layer. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change who gets to stay generalist and who is forced to become legible to the machine. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, repair culture stops looking like a gadget and starts looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. That difference is easy to underrate because it does not show up cleanly in a benchmark. But mood is one of the outputs. If a system consistently narrows attention, thickens bureaucracy, or turns daily life into a sequence of small permissions, that is not accidental collateral. It is part of what the product is. I have never been persuaded by arguments that treat human texture as a sentimental side issue next to performance.

Looking back from May 2019, I could already feel my own position shifting. I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “On solar abundance as a rehearsal for a different kind of city,” from January 2019. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. Here I was more interested in what happens after the applause: the maintenance burden, the political assumptions, the quiet social sorting, the emotional contract being drafted between human beings and the systems now arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. It was just incomplete in a way optimism often is when the machinery has not yet touched enough lives.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. We can admire good tools without surrendering the right to ask what world they are quietly assembling around themselves. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. It begins by refusing the oversized story until the smaller one proves it deserves to grow. It is one of the reasons I keep a blog at all.

A final note to myself from May 2019: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

title: A note on human-scale robotics as an art project with civic consequences
summary: A personal essay about human-scale robotics, and the longer argument hidden inside the gap between wonder and maintenance.
topic: human-scale robotics as an art project with civic consequences
body: My notes on human-scale robotics began with a small irritation in April 2019. Everyone around me seemed to agree that the big story was obvious, and the agreement itself made me uneasy. When consensus arrives too quickly, I start looking for whatever got edited out to make the sentence so clean. In this case the omitted piece was the gap between wonder and maintenance. That phrase may sound abstract, but on the ground it shows up as ordinary details: who has to maintain the thing, who absorbs its errors, who gets asked to trust it first, and who benefits from calling that trust progress.

Human-scale robotics appealed to me because it sat right at the border between practical engineering and cultural fantasy. The engineering part is easier to talk about, because it turns into parts, systems, metrics, and demos. The fantasy part is harder, but it matters just as much. Every tool arrives carrying an image of the person who will use it and the society it hopes to stabilize. If you listen carefully, you can hear those assumptions in the design choices. The question is never just whether a system works. It is also what kind of behavior, dependency, and mood the system quietly normalizes while it works.

What I kept seeing was a gap between prototype conditions and public conditions. In the prototype, everyone knows they are participating in an experiment. In public, people inherit the result as environment. That difference changes the moral burden of design. It is one thing to test a tool among enthusiasts who volunteered for weirdness. It is another to fold the same logic into schools, offices, hospitals, transit, or housing and expect gratitude because the interface looks modern. The gap between wonder and maintenance becomes sharper whenever a niche tool tries to skip directly into normal life without surviving a long middle period of explanation and repair.

What changed most was not my taste for the topic but my threshold for abstraction. After writing “On solar abundance as a rehearsal for a different kind of city,” I found myself less willing to accept big words without a scene attached to them. If a claim about the future cannot survive contact with somebody's day, their tools, their fatigue, and their incentives, I do not trust the claim to survive for long at all. That has become one of my small private tests. Ask the sentence to descend from altitude. If it lands gracefully, keep it. If it panics on impact, start over.

What I wanted, and still want, is not purity but proportion. Let the tools be useful. Let them even be strange. But let us stay attentive to the conditions under which usefulness turns into dependence and strangeness gets marketed as destiny. Human-scale robotics seemed worth writing about because it carried that tension in plain view. The future is rarely a single invention. More often it is a style of relationship that becomes normal before we have finished deciding whether we like it.

A final note to myself from April 2019: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. I have become less interested in winning the argument about what comes next than in noticing what a given tool seems to ask of the people nearest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: In Dust We Trust

title: In Dust We Trust
summary: A first post from Burning Man 2018, where robot fantasies only felt worth keeping if they could survive the dust and still point toward a livable world.
topic: Burning Man 2018, robots, sustainability, and civic imagination
body: I came home from Burning Man 2018 with alkaline dust in my shoes, a sore throat, and one stubborn thought I could not shake: if we are going to let machines organize more of human life, they should at least earn the right to do it in a world that still feels playful. That year the robot theme was impossible to miss. Everywhere you looked there were chrome costumes, handmade exoskeletons, animatronic contraptions, solar creatures wobbling through whiteout conditions, and people talking about automation as if it might become either our servant or our final mirror. The mood was not efficiency. It was theater, curiosity, and a strangely sincere hope that technology could still be intimate rather than managerial.

What I loved about the desert version of robotics was how obviously provisional it all was. A machine broke and five strangers crouched around it with zip ties, a bike light, and some wild theory about torque. Nobody there believed the robot was magic. It was a contraption in a temporary city built by people who knew maintenance was part of the fantasy. That matters to me. The future I want is not one where robots replace us so cleanly that the human part disappears. I want a future where the burden of dull work gets lighter, where energy gets cleaner, where logistics feel less cruel, and where the systems around us are designed with enough humility that ordinary people can still understand the seams.

The harder question is whether that future can stay joyful once it becomes scalable. It is easy to adore a robot when it is dust-covered, solar-powered, and clearly part art project. It is harder when the same logic arrives as surveillance, labor discipline, or a dashboard optimized by someone who has never been hot, tired, and running late. The desert is useful because it exaggerates values. If something is wasteful, flimsy, or fake-generous, the playa reveals it quickly. Sustainability is not a slogan out there. You feel it in water, battery life, shade, repair, and whether the thing you built can survive contact with wind and exhaustion. I wish more of mainstream technology had to pass through that kind of honesty.

My optimistic version of robotics still looks weirdly simple. Let the machines do the repetitive hauling, the dangerous inspection, the midnight sorting, the ugly heat. Let them make cities quieter, cleaner, and easier to share. Let them help us produce abundance without producing the usual giant pile of waste. But if they are going to run more of the world, then the world they run should be worth inheriting. It should be sustainable first, legible second, and beautiful almost by accident. It should not feel like we traded one extractive system for a more frictionless one. I do not want the smart city as a mall with sensors. I want the smart city as a place where people have more time, less drudgery, and more reasons to trust each other.

The phrase that kept circling in my head all week was in dust we trust. Not because dust is noble, but because it is democratic. It gets on everything. It levels expensive and handmade objects alike. It reminds you that no machine is above its environment. That may be the principle I want to keep. Build robots, yes. Build software, sure. But build them in a way that admits they live among bodies, weather, repair cycles, limits, and actual communities. If a machine cannot survive a little dust without becoming authoritarian about it, I am not convinced it belongs in charge of much.

So that is where I am starting this blog: not with a polished thesis, but with a scene. A temporary city. Robot costumes at sunset. Solar chargers humming like a promise. A lot of very smart people still acting as if delight matters. Maybe that is naive. Maybe a few years from now I will read this back and find it impossibly earnest. But I would rather begin with the possibility that technology could serve a livable world than begin with cynicism and call it maturity. If the future is going to be automated, I want to keep asking the same question until it becomes annoying: automated toward what kind of life?
